slides.title: The No-Clones Club ⛔
slides.content:
An ArrayList happily allows duplicate data. `[5, 5, 5, 5]` is totally fine.

But sometimes, you need a collection of entirely unique items. For example, storing the IP Addresses of users who visited your site today. If the same user refreshes 10 times, you only want their IP stored ONCE.

Use a **HashSet**.

slides.title: Automatic Filtering 🚰
slides.content: A HashSet looks just like an ArrayList, but it silently ignores any attempts to add a duplicate value.

quizData.question: If you want a collection of names, and you DO NOT care if there are duplicate names, but you DO care about keeping them in the exact order they were added, which should you use?
quizData.explanation: ArrayList maintains the sequence/index order perfectly and allows duplicates. HashSets do NOT guarantee ordering at all!

slides.title: The Final Rule of Collections 📚
slides.content:
You now know the Big Three:

1. **ArrayList**: Ordered sequentially. Duplicates allowed.
2. **HashSet**: Order not guaranteed. Duplicates forbidden.
3. **HashMap**: Stores data in Key-Value pairs. Keys must be unique.

Mastering when to use which structure is what separates beginners from professionals!
